title: Preparing pigs for the abattoir: the final weeks
subtitle: What to do in the last 4 weeks before slaughter day.
excerpt: The 4 weeks before an abattoir booking shape meat quality, ease of handling on the day, and your legal compliance. Pigs that are handled calmly and regularly load easily; pigs that have never been walked or moved in groups may injure themselves or the handler under the stress of transport. The preparation starts now, not the night before.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Pigs at Slaughter (2003). AHDB Pork, pre-slaughter handling and welfare guidance, ahdb.org.uk. British Pig Association, smallholder guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: The minimum time between the last administration of a veterinary medicine and when the animal can be slaughtered for food. Varies by product; must be observed to avoid illegal residues in the meat. Recorded in the food chain information document.

A legal document that must accompany pigs to slaughter, declaring any veterinary treatments received in the past 60 days, the holding CPH, and a declaration that withdrawal periods have been respected.

content.text: A pig going to slaughter at around 80 to 90 kg liveweight should have been building to that weight on a consistent finisher ration since around 60 kg. The final month is when to confirm the booking, check
on any treatments given, and start the handling practice that will make loading day go smoothly.
Four weeks out: booking and compliance

Book the abattoir slot. Most small abattoirs serving smallholders book 4 to 6 weeks ahead; popular dates around Christmas fill early. Confirm the killing weight requirement, most abattoirs have a minimum and a preferred range; pigs significantly overweight add backfat and reduce yield. Check withdrawal periods on any wormers, vaccines, or antibiotics administered in the last 60 days and record them for the
. Administer no new treatments unless medically necessary within the abattoir's minimum withdrawal window.
Walk through the pen with the pigs daily using a
to guide them from one side to the other. Pigs that have never been moved with a board panic when confronted with one on transport day; a few practice sessions habituate them to controlled movement.

Continue normal feeding up to the final day. Withhold food for 12 hours before the journey to reduce gut fill and the risk of regurgitation under stress. Do not reduce water, dehydrated pigs produce poorer-quality pork (pale, soft, exudative meat) and are more prone to collapse under handling stress.
Day before: FCI document and loading plan

Complete the food chain information document the day before. This must be sent to the abattoir in advance (email is acceptable) or handed over on arrival. Prepare the loading route: remove any hazards in the path from pen to trailer, ensure the trailer ramp is stable and not slippery. Pigs load best in the early morning before heat builds; plan accordingly.
